title: Worktree cleanup can throw and mask the real failure
reasoning: The command calls cleanupWorktree() both in normal error paths and again inside the catch block without guarding cleanup failures. If addWorktree, green(), snapshotting, implementer execution, or artifact writing throws, then cleanupWorktree() in the catch can also throw and reject runChangeCostCommand instead of returning the RuntimeResult promised by its API. The same risk exists in the baseline-red branch, where cleanupWorktree() is called before returning. This makes bad error handling observable to callers and can hide the original failure message.
reproduction: Make removeWorktree fail during the catch path, for example by using a mocked @codenuke/substrate removeWorktree that throws after any earlier operation throws. runChangeCostCommand will reject instead of resolving { exitCode: 1, ... }.
recommendation: Wrap cleanupWorktree() calls in a best-effort helper that catches cleanup errors and appends them to stderr, while preserving the original failure return. Use that helper everywhere cleanup is part of an error/return path.
whyTestsDoNotAlreadyCoverThis: The included tests only exercise tokenize, lcsEditSize, editCost, verifyCost, buildImplementerPrompt, costOf, and meanChangeCost; they do not cover runChangeCostCommand or cleanup failure paths.
suggestedRegressionTest: Mock removeWorktree to throw during a runChangeCostCommand failure path and assert the function resolves with exitCode 1 and includes the original error rather than rejecting.
minimumFixScope: Add guarded cleanup handling inside runChangeCostCommand and update runtime tests for failure cleanup behavior.
